newsletter.subject: fireflies gathered at the edge of may
newsletter.title: when spring holds its breath
newsletter.opening: It's 11:17pm and miso is stationed by the screen door, watching the first fireflies of the season rise from our yard like tiny prayers. The air has that perfect May warmth — not the heavy weight of summer yet, just the soft promise of longer days settling over everything. I'm curled in the window seat with my guitar, following the melody of this moment.
newsletter.section1_body:
There's something profound about these threshold moments — when May hasn't quite decided if it's still spring or already reaching toward summer. The fireflies know though. They emerge with such quiet confidence, as if they've been waiting in the grass all month for exactly this temperature, this quality of dusk light.

I've been thinking about how we carry these seasonal shifts in our bodies. The way my shoulders finally dropped last week when the evening air stopped biting. How miso started choosing the cool kitchen tiles over the sunny spots by the window. These small migrations we make within the same four walls, following some ancient knowing about what our souls need.

Maybe that's what draws me to these liminal hours — 11pm when the day is releasing its grip, May when the earth is stretching into its full self. There's permission in the in-between spaces. Permission to be neither fully awake nor asleep, neither the person we were in winter nor the one we're becoming in the long light of summer.
newsletter.quote: some evenings arrive like a gentle hand on your shoulder, saying rest now
newsletter.section2_body:
While I was getting lost in the complexity of being human, miso simply positioned herself in the perfect spot to witness something beautiful. No analysis, no wondering about meaning — just presence. Just the soft acknowledgment that fireflies rising from grass deserve attention.

So I put down my thoughts and picked up my guitar instead. Let my fingers find that melody that's been hiding at the edges all week — the one that sounds like warm air and distant thunder and the first brave insects of the season. Sometimes the wisest thing we can do is stop thinking our way through the magic and just sit with it instead.
newsletter.prompt: What season is your inner world in right now, and what is it asking you to notice?
newsletter.closing: The fireflies are settling deeper into the yard now, their lights becoming a quiet constellation just for us. I hope you have your own gentle witnesses tonight — whether they're glowing insects or purring cats or simply the steady rhythm of your own breathing. The world holds space for all your seasons, and it's okay to be exactly where you are.
newsletter.ps: miso just yawned so big I could see all her tiny teeth. bedtime calls.
newsletter.note_wednesday: Found miso sleeping in a patch of afternoon sun that somehow made it through three layers of blinds. The dedication of cats to finding perfect light never fails to amaze me.
newsletter.note_friday: The first fireflies appeared last night and I immediately knew what next week's letter would be about. Some moments choose themselves.

youtube_description.title: for introverts who find magic in quiet may evenings 🌙
youtube_description.body: There's something about these perfect May evenings that calls us to slow down and witness the small magic happening right outside our windows. Whether you're watching fireflies rise from grass or just letting the warm air remind you that gentler days are here, this is your soundtrack for settling into the sweetness of late spring.
youtube_description.song_line: ✨ drift into firefly season to Chasing Fireflies: https://youtu.be/7SXAG51wZmY
